walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Scrabble set up. First, unfold the game board and lay it flat in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. You will see a fifteen by fifteen grid of squares. Most squares are plain, but some are colored. Light blue squares are Double Letter Score. Dark blue squares are Triple Letter Score. Pink squares are Double Word Score. And red squares are Triple Word Score. Right in the very center of the board, you will see a star. That center star counts as a Double Word Score.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Now find the cloth drawstring bag and all one hundred letter tiles. Drop every tile into the bag and give it a thorough shake to mix them up. Each tile has a letter printed on it along with a small number showing its point value. There are also two blank tiles that act as wildcards and are worth zero points. Keep the bag in the center of the table where everyone can draw from it easily.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Tile Racks
walkthrough.text: Give each player one tile rack. These are the long wooden or plastic stands with a groove for holding tiles upright. Place your rack in front of you so only you can see the letters. You will always have up to seven tiles on your rack during the game.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Let's figure out who goes first. Each player draws one tile from the bag without looking. The player who draws the letter closest to A goes first. A blank tile beats everything. If two players tie, those players draw again. Once you have determined the order, return those tiles to the bag and give it another good shake.

walkthrough.title: Draw Starting Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now each player draws seven tiles from the bag without peeking. Place them on your rack so only you can see them. Keep your tiles hidden from the other players. This is your starting hand. You will always draw back up to seven tiles after playing a word.

walkthrough.title: Agree on a Dictionary
walkthrough.text: Before you start playing, agree on which dictionary you will use for challenges. In North America, the standard is the Official Scrabble Players Dictionary. Internationally, it is Collins Scrabble Words. For a casual game, any dictionary app or website works fine. The important thing is that everyone agrees on the same source before the first word is played.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: That is it for setup. The board is out, tiles are in the bag, everyone has seven tiles on their rack, and you know who goes first. The first player must place their opening word so that at least one letter covers the center star. Remember, that star is a Double Word Score, so the first word gets its total doubled automatically. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Place 15x15 board in center of table

Center star = Double Word Score

Includes 2 blank (wild) tiles worth 0 pts

bullets: First word must cover the center star

walkthrough.title: Three Turn Options
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you have three choices. You can play a word on the board, exchange tiles from your rack, or simply pass. Most turns you will play a word, but sometimes your letters are so bad that exchanging or passing is the smarter move. Let's go through each option.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Word
walkthrough.text: To play a word, place one or more tiles from your rack onto the board. All tiles you place in a single turn must go in one straight line, either across a row or down a column. There cannot be any gaps between your tiles, though existing tiles on the board can fill spaces in between. After the very first word, every new word must connect to at least one tile that is already on the board.

walkthrough.title: Cross-Words Must Be Valid
walkthrough.text: Here is a rule that trips up new players. When you place tiles, you might form new words in the perpendicular direction as well. Every single word that gets created by your placement, both the main word and any side words, must be a valid dictionary word. If even one of those cross-words is not a real word, the entire play is invalid.

walkthrough.title: Scoring a Word
walkthrough.text: Add up the point values of all the letters in each new word you formed. If you placed a tile on a Double Letter Score square, that tile's value is doubled. Triple Letter Score triples that one tile. If any tile in your word sits on a Double Word Score, the entire word's total is doubled after adding up all the letters. Triple Word Score triples the whole word. Premium squares only count on the turn the tile is first placed on them. After that turn, the square is just a regular space.

walkthrough.title: Multiple Words in One Turn
walkthrough.text: If your placement creates more than one word, you score all of them. This happens often with parallel plays. For example, placing a word next to an existing word can form several two-letter cross-words at the same time. Each new word scores separately, and premium squares apply to every word they help form on that turn.

walkthrough.title: The Bingo Bonus
walkthrough.text: If you manage to use all seven tiles from your rack in a single turn, you earn a fifty-point bonus on top of whatever the word scores. This is called a bingo. Bingos are rare but game-changing. Even a modest seven-letter word with the bingo bonus can score over seventy points in one turn.

walkthrough.title: Using Blank Tiles
walkthrough.text: The two blank tiles are wildcards. When you play a blank, you declare which letter it represents. That letter is permanent for the rest of the game. A blank tile always scores zero points, even if it sits on a premium square. Blanks are extremely valuable because they let you form words you otherwise could not spell, especially for bingos.

walkthrough.title: Drawing New Tiles
walkthrough.text: After you play a word, draw tiles from the bag until you have seven on your rack again. If the bag runs out, you just play with however many tiles you have left. Your turn is over once you have drawn, and play passes clockwise to the next player.

walkthrough.title: Exchanging Tiles
walkthrough.text: If your rack is terrible, you can spend your turn exchanging tiles instead of playing a word. Pick any number of tiles from your rack and set them face down. Draw the same number of new tiles from the bag. Then mix the old tiles back in. You score zero points for this turn, and you cannot play a word on the same turn you exchange. There must be at least seven tiles left in the bag to exchange.

walkthrough.title: Challenging a Word
walkthrough.text: After someone plays a word but before the next player takes their turn, any player can challenge the word. Look it up in your agreed dictionary. If the word is invalid, the player takes back their tiles, scores zero, and loses that turn. If the word is actually valid, the challenger may lose their next turn depending on your house rules. In casual games, many groups skip the penalty for failed challenges.

walkthrough.title: How the Game Ends
walkthrough.text: The game ends in one of two ways. Either the tile bag is empty and one player uses their last tile, or all players pass their turns consecutively because nobody can or wants to play. The player who goes out first gets a bonus equal to the total point value of all tiles remaining on opponents' racks. Every other player subtracts the value of their unplayed tiles from their own score.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The player with the highest total score wins. In casual games, scores typically land between two hundred and three hundred fifty points. In competitive play, scores can reach four hundred or higher. If there is a tie, the player with the higher score before the endgame adjustment wins.

summary.title: Three Turn Options
bullets: Play a word on the board
Exchange tiles (score 0, uses full turn)
Pass (score 0, change nothing)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Practice
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns of a two-player game between Player 1 and Player 2. We will see good plays, common mistakes, and the lessons they teach. Follow along even if you have more players at your table. Ready? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: The Opening Racks
walkthrough.text: Both players have drawn their seven tiles. Player 1 has the letters H, O, U, S, E, R, and T. That is a fantastic rack with a good mix of vowels and consonants. Player 2 has Q, U, I, V, A, L, and E. Not bad, but that Q could be tricky later.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Opening Word
walkthrough.text: Player 1 goes first and plays HOUSE across the center star. That is five letters, covering the center square. Let's score it. H is four, O is one, U is one, S is one, E is one. That is eight points, but the center star is a Double Word Score, so the total doubles to sixteen. Nice opening. Player 1 draws five new tiles from the bag.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Opening
walkthrough.text: HOUSE was a strong opening for two reasons. First, it used five tiles, which means Player 1 drew five fresh tiles and has more options next turn. Second, it created multiple hooking opportunities. Other players can add an S to make HOUSES, or build off the H, O, U, S, or E in perpendicular directions. Opening the board invites interaction, which is part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: The Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 2 looks at their rack and spots the Q. They panic and immediately play QI for two letters, using the I from HOUSE. QI is a valid word worth eleven points. But Player 2 also had VEIL available going perpendicular through the E in HOUSE, which would have scored more and used up more tiles. Player 2 played the Q just to get rid of it, instead of looking for their best scoring option.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Panic Over the Q
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is do not let one scary tile dictate your whole turn. The Q is worth ten points, which is great when you use it well. QI and QOPH and QADI are all valid without a U. But dumping the Q at the first opportunity often means you miss a higher-scoring play with your other tiles. Scan your full rack for the best overall play, not just the one that gets rid of a problem tile.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws into some nice letters and plays ROAST perpendicular to the S in HOUSE. The R lands on a Double Letter Score, so that R is worth two instead of one. The word scores twelve points. Player 1 is building a steady lead by making clean, connected plays.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Wasting the S
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws new tiles and plays SAT for just five points. They used their S tile to start a short, low-scoring word. The S is one of the most valuable tiles in Scrabble because you can hook it onto almost any existing word to form a plural while simultaneously building a perpendicular word. Playing SAT for five points when that S could have been a hook for twenty or more is a costly mistake.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Guard Your S Tiles
walkthrough.text: Never use an S tile unless it earns you at least ten extra points beyond what you could score without it. The S is a hook. Add it to HOUSE to make HOUSES while playing a new word through it going the other direction. That way you score both words in one turn. There are only four S tiles in the bag, so treat each one like gold.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Hooks and Scores Big
walkthrough.text: Player 1 demonstrates the right way to use positioning. They add letters parallel to ROAST, forming three two-letter cross-words simultaneously. Each cross-word is valid, and the combined score is twenty-two points. This is called a parallel play, and it is one of the most powerful techniques in Scrabble.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Cross-Words
walkthrough.text: Player 2 tries a parallel play of their own but forgets to check the cross-words. They place VALE next to an existing word, but one of the two-letter combinations they create is not a valid word. Player 1 challenges, and the play is invalid. Player 2 takes back the tiles and scores zero for the turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Cross-Words
walkthrough.text: Before you commit to a play, check every new word your tiles create, including the small two-letter words formed perpendicular to your main word. Memorizing common two-letter words like QI, ZA, XI, JO, and AA goes a long way. If you are not sure a cross-word is valid, play a different word or use a different position.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Recovers
walkthrough.text: Player 2 takes a breath and looks at the board more carefully. This time they find a spot where QUIVER fits perfectly, landing the Q on a Triple Letter Score. That Q is now worth thirty points by itself. The full word scores forty-two points and vaults Player 2 back into contention. Patience and board vision pay off.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Board Awareness
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and look at the board. Several premium squares are still open, especially the Triple Word Score corners. Both players should be thinking about whether their next play opens or blocks access to those high-value squares. Placing a word near a Triple Word without being able to use it yourself is an invitation for your opponent to score big.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how hooking, parallel plays, and premium square awareness separate strong plays from weak ones. You have also seen three common mistakes: panicking over the Q, wasting an S tile, and forgetting to check cross-words.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to premium squares, tile values, and key two-letter words.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → tile values, premium squares, 2-letter words
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Balance Your Rack
walkthrough.text: The single most important habit in Scrabble is keeping a balanced rack. Aim for roughly three consonants and three or four vowels at all times. If you end up with five vowels and two consonants, or vice versa, your word options shrink dramatically. When choosing which word to play, think about which letters you are leaving behind. If your play dumps three consonants but leaves you with all vowels, consider a different word that keeps your rack more balanced.

walkthrough.title: Learn Key Two-Letter Words
walkthrough.text: Two-letter words are the secret weapon of Scrabble. They let you make parallel plays, squeeze into tight spots, and hook off existing tiles. The official word list has over one hundred valid two-letter words. You do not need to memorize all of them, but knowing the high-value ones is essential. Start with QI, ZA, XI, XU, JO, and KA. Then learn the vowel pairs like AA, AE, AI, OE, and OI. Even knowing twenty of them will dramatically expand your options.

walkthrough.title: Premium Square Strategy
walkthrough.text: Always scan the board for premium squares before deciding where to play. A mediocre word on a Triple Word Score beats a good word on plain squares. But be careful about opening premium squares for your opponent. If you play a word that ends one space away from a Triple Word Score, your opponent might extend through your word to grab it. Think defensively too. Sometimes the smartest play is blocking an opponent's access to a premium square rather than chasing your own score.

walkthrough.title: Tile Management
walkthrough.text: Treat certain tiles as premium assets. The S tiles let you hook plurals for double scores. The blank tiles are your best shot at a bingo. Hold onto these tiles unless using them nets you a significant point advantage, at least ten extra points for an S and ideally a bingo for a blank. On the flip side, get rid of duplicate letters quickly. Having three of the same letter clogs your rack and limits your word options.

walkthrough.title: When to Exchange
walkthrough.text: Do not be afraid to exchange tiles. If your rack is full of duplicates, unplayable consonant clusters, or all vowels with no good words in sight, exchanging is often worth more than forcing a weak five-point play. A good rule of thumb is: if you cannot score at least twenty points, consider exchanging. The new tiles might set you up for a much bigger play next turn. Just remember, you cannot exchange if fewer than seven tiles remain in the bag.

walkthrough.title: Hooking for Double Scores
walkthrough.text: Hooking is one of the easiest ways to boost your score. Add a single letter to the front or back of an existing word to form a new word while simultaneously building your main word perpendicular to it. For example, add an S to CART to make CARTS while spelling a new word downward starting with that S. You score both words in one turn. Look for hookable words on the board every turn.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Awareness
walkthrough.text: As the tile bag empties, the game shifts. Start tracking which high-value tiles have not appeared yet. If the Q, Z, X, or J are still unplayed, someone is holding them. In the endgame, try to go out first. The player who empties their rack adds all opponents' remaining tile values to their score, which can be a twenty-point swing. Avoid getting stuck with high-value tiles like Q or Z at the end.

walkthrough.title: Have Fun with Words
walkthrough.text: Scrabble rewards curiosity about language. Pay attention to unusual words that come up during games. Keep a mental or physical list of words that surprised you. Every game you play adds to your vocabulary. Do not stress about playing the perfect word every turn. Even experienced players miss optimal plays constantly. The goal is to enjoy the puzzle and gradually improve over time.

summary.title: Have Fun with Words

Every game expands your vocabulary
Don't stress perfection — enjoy the puzzle

walkthrough.title: Bingo Hunting
walkthrough.text: The fifty-point bingo bonus is the biggest single scoring opportunity in Scrabble. To hunt for bingos, keep your rack loaded with common bingo-friendly letters. The most flexible letters for seven-letter words are the blank, S, E, R, A, N, T, I, and L. If you have five or six of these letters, consider exchanging the remaining one or two tiles to fish for a bingo rather than playing a mediocre word.

walkthrough.title: Rack Leave Strategy
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about rack leave, which means the tiles remaining on your rack after a play. A word that scores thirty points but leaves you with Q, V, and two Us is worse than a word that scores twenty but leaves you with E, R, and S. Before committing to a play, check what tiles you will have left. Good rack leave sets you up for strong plays on future turns. Bad rack leave creates cascading problems that take multiple turns to fix.

walkthrough.title: Board Control: Open vs. Closed
walkthrough.text: Controlling the board is as important as scoring. An open board has many places to connect new words and reach premium squares. A closed board has few openings and limits both players' options. When you are ahead, close the board down. Play words that do not extend toward premium squares. When you are behind, open the board up by creating new hook points and lines toward Triple Word Scores. Board control is how you protect a lead or engineer a comeback.

walkthrough.title: Parallel Play Mastery
walkthrough.text: Parallel plays are one of the highest-scoring techniques in Scrabble. Instead of building perpendicular to an existing word, place your word directly alongside it. Every adjacent letter pair must form a valid two-letter word. A five-letter word placed parallel to another five-letter word can score both the main word and five cross-words in a single turn. This requires solid two-letter word knowledge but can yield forty to sixty points from seemingly modest tiles.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Tile Tracking
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, start tracking which tiles have been played. There are exact counts for every letter in the bag. If you have seen three of the four S tiles, you know only one S remains. If the Z, X, and both blanks have appeared, you can estimate opponent rack composition. In the endgame, exact tile tracking lets you calculate your opponent's possible plays and block their best options.

walkthrough.title: Q Without U
walkthrough.text: The Q is worth ten points but requires careful management. Learn the valid Q-without-U words so you are never stuck. QI, QOPH, QANAT, QADI, QAID, QAIDS, QIVIUT, and MBAQANGA are all playable in competitive Scrabble. When you draw the Q, look for these options first. If the board allows QI on a premium square, it can be one of the highest-scoring two-letter plays in the game.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Calculation
walkthrough.text: In the endgame, when the bag is empty, you can deduce your opponent's exact tiles by tracking what has been played. This lets you calculate their best possible score and play defensively to prevent it. Sometimes scoring fewer points on your turn while blocking a fifty-point opponent play is the winning move. Endgame calculation is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Turn Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Every tile you play is a tempo investment. Playing two tiles for ten points is worse tempo than playing five tiles for twenty points, because the five-tile play cycles more of your rack and draws more fresh tiles. High-tempo plays keep your rack fresh and increase the chance of drawing into bingo combinations. Think of your score not just in points per turn but in points per tile played.

bullets: 50-pt bingo = biggest single scoring opportunity

Exchange 1-2 bad tiles to fish for bingo when rack is close

summary.title: Rack Leave Strategy
bullets: Evaluate tiles remaining after a play, not just points scored
30 pts with Q, V, U, U leave < 20 pts with E, R, S leave
Good leave → strong future turns; bad leave → cascading problems

summary.title: Board Control: Open vs. Closed

Behind → open board (create hooks toward TW/DW squares)
Board control protects leads and enables comebacks

Learn these to avoid getting stuck with Q at game end

summary.title: Endgame Calculation

summary.title: Tempo and Turn Efficiency
bullets: More tiles played → more fresh draws → better bingo chances
5 tiles for 20 pts > 2 tiles for 10 pts (better tempo)
Think points per tile, not just points per turn

entries.definition: Light blue square. The tile placed here has its letter value doubled. Only counts on the turn it is first placed.
Dark blue square. The tile placed here has its letter value tripled. Only counts on the turn it is first placed.
Pink square. After summing all letter values, the entire word's score is doubled. Only counts on the turn a tile is first placed on it.
Red square. After summing all letter values, the entire word's score is tripled. Only counts on the turn a tile is first placed on it.
The square at the exact center of the board (row 8, column 8). Functions as a Double Word Score. The first word played must cover this square.
Using all seven tiles from your rack in a single turn. Earns a 50-point bonus on top of the normal word score. Also called a bonus in some regions.

Adding a single letter to the beginning or end of an existing word to form a new valid word. For example, adding S to CART to make CARTS. The hook letter usually also starts or ends a perpendicular word for double scoring.
Placing a word directly alongside an existing word so that every adjacent letter pair forms a valid two-letter word. Scores the main word plus all cross-words in a single turn.
Any new word formed perpendicular to your main placement. All cross-words must be valid dictionary words. Cross-words are scored individually.
The tiles remaining on your rack after making a play. Advanced players consider rack leave quality when choosing between multiple scoring options.
A wildcard tile that can represent any letter. Worth 0 points in all cases. Once placed, the declared letter is permanent for the rest of the game. Two blanks in the standard set.
Spending your turn to swap any number of rack tiles for new ones from the bag. Scores 0 points. Cannot be combined with playing a word. Requires at least 7 tiles in the bag.

Disputing the validity of a played word by looking it up in the agreed dictionary. If invalid, the player removes tiles and scores 0. If valid, the challenger may lose their next turn (varies by house rules).
Playing your last tile when the bag is empty. The player who goes out adds the total value of all opponents' remaining tiles to their score. Triggers the end of the game.
E, A, I, O, N, R, T, L, S, U are each worth 1 point. These are the most common letters in the bag.
D and G are 2 points. B, C, M, P are 3 points. F, H, V, W, Y are 4 points. K is 5 points.
J and X are 8 points. Q and Z are 10 points. These are the highest-value tiles and the hardest to play.

Essential valid 2-letter words for advanced play: QI (Q without U), ZA (pizza), XI and XU (high-value X plays), JO, KA, AA, AE, AI, OE, OI, and many more. Knowing these unlocks parallel plays and tight-spot placements.
Valid words containing Q but not U: QI, QOPH, QANAT, QADI, QAID, QAIDS, QIVIUT, MBAQANGA, and others. Essential knowledge to avoid being stuck with Q at game end.
A board state with many available connection points and accessible premium squares. Favors the player who is behind, as it creates more high-scoring opportunities.
A board state with few connection points and blocked premium squares. Favors the player who is ahead, as it limits comeback opportunities.
